title: Monochromatic Sums and Products over the Rationals
speaker: Maria-Romina Ivan
abstract:
Hindman’s Theorem states that whenever the natural numbers are finitely coloured there exists an infinite sequence all of whose finite sums are the same colour. By considering just powers of 2, this immediately implies the corresponding result for products: whenever the naturals are finitely coloured there exists a sequence all of whose products are the same colour.
But what happens if we ask for both the sums and the products to all have the same colour? It turns out that this is not true: it has been known since the 1970s that there is a finite colouring of the naturals for which no infinite sequence has the set of all of its sums and products monochromatic.
In this talk we will investigate what happens to this question if we move from the naturals to a larger space such as the dyadic rationals, the rationals, or even the reals.
Joint work with Neil Hindman and Imre Leader.

proof_stages.label: Ramsey-theoretic background and the natural-number obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up finite colorings and monochromatic additive/multiplicative structure, reviews Hindman's finite-sums theorem and the finite-products analogue over the naturals, and states that over the naturals a finite coloring can already destroy monochromatic pairwise sums and products.

proof_stages.label: Larger ambient spaces and the minimal-base obstruction theorem
proof_stages.summary: After explaining why dyadic rationals and ordinary finite base expansions suggest but do not solve the rational problem, the speaker defines the bounded-prime classes T_k/minimal base and states the main rational result: there is a finite coloring of Q such that any monochromatic finite-sums-and-products sequence must involve infinitely many denominator primes.

proof_stages.label: Auxiliary real coloring and reduction to zero or infinity
proof_stages.summary: A second coloring theorem for the reals rules out monochromatic pairwise sums and products for sequences that are both bounded and bounded away from zero, reducing the bounded-denominator-prime case to sequences that either go to zero or become unbounded.

proof_stages.label: Pair-coloring lemma for start and end data
proof_stages.summary: The central combinatorial lemma is formulated as a finite coloring of pairs of natural numbers that forbids increasing start/end sequences whose mixed pairs and coordinatewise sums are all monochromatic, matching the start and end behavior of base expansions under sums and products.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of the zero-convergent rational case to the pair lemma
proof_stages.summary: For a sequence x_n tending to zero with bounded denominator primes, one passes to a subsequence with a fixed minimal base and disjoint supports, so that starts and ends of x_n+x_m and x_nx_m feed into the pair-coloring lemma; this coloring is combined with the auxiliary real coloring to eliminate all T_k at once.

proof_stages.label: Five-coordinate binary coloring of pairs
proof_stages.summary: To prove the pair-coloring lemma, the speaker defines a coloring of a pair (a,b) using the parity of the final binary position of a and b, the digit immediately before each final 1, and whether the binary supports of a and b are left-to-right disjoint.

proof_stages.label: Contradiction via binary carries and interleaving supports
proof_stages.summary: Assuming a monochromatic forbidden configuration, the first four coloring coordinates force no two a_n and no two b_n to end at the same binary position, allowing disjoint-support subsequences, while the fifth coordinate then distinguishes pairs (a_1,b_n) from summed pairs (a_1+a_n,b_1+b_n), giving the contradiction.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and open directions
proof_stages.summary: The argument is summarized as using only the beginning and end of finite expansions, suggesting that progress on the full rational question may require an analogue for factorial-base expansions, and the same pair-coloring idea is noted to give a short proof of the natural-number pairwise obstruction.

examples.name: 2-adic valuation coloring against repeated sums
examples.note: In response to a question about allowing repetitions in Hindman's theorem, the speaker colors integers by the parity of the largest power of 2 dividing them to show why repeated summands would break the statement.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker describes the valuation-mod-2 coloring and checks that x_n and 2x_n get different colors when repetitions are allowed

passes.reason: the coloring by v2(n) mod 2 makes x and 2x have different colors, refuting the repeated-sums variant
the parity-of-2-adic-valuation coloring is an ordinary structured coloring used to refute Hindman's theorem with repeated summands
the v2(n) mod 2 coloring is a standard valuation-based coloring used to refute Hindman-style repeated finite sums
The coloring by parity of the 2-adic valuation is used to refute Hindman-style finite sums when repetitions are allowed.
The v2-parity coloring is an algebraically regular 2-adic coloring used to disprove Hindman's theorem with repeated summands.
the coloring by the parity of the 2-adic valuation is used to show the repeated-summand version of Hindman's theorem fails.
the v2(n) mod 2 coloring is used to refute the repeated-sums variant of Hindman's theorem.
It is used to refute the repeated-sums version of Hindman, while the 2-adic valuation parity coloring is a nontrivial but systematic ordinary coloring.
the parity of the 2-adic valuation is used as a bad coloring showing Hindman's finite-sums statement fails if repeated summands are allowed

The 2-adic valuation parity coloring is used to refute Hindman's theorem with repeated summands, while the coloring itself is a standard algebraic construction rather than a sporadic, extremal, or degenerate object.

examples.name: Encoding products by powers of two
examples.note: The finite-products version over the naturals is illustrated by recoloring n according to the color of 2^n, so Hindman finite sums of exponents become finite products of powers of two.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly runs the powers-of-two encoding, recoloring n by the color of 2^n and translating finite sums into finite products

passes.reason: the powers of two form a highly regular geometric coding used constructively to turn sums of exponents into products
the powers-of-two encoding is a standard embedding turning sums of exponents into products, used constructively rather than adversarially
the powers 2^n form a highly regular geometric sequence used positively to convert sums of exponents into products
Powers of two are used constructively to convert additive finite-sums structure into multiplicative finite-products structure.
The powers of two form a perfectly regular geometric multiplicative subsemigroup used positively to encode products as sums.
powers of two are used as a standard systematic coding where sums of exponents become products, giving a positive construction rather than a failure case.
powers of two form a rank-one subsemigroup collapsing products to sums for a positive transfer argument.
It is a positive reduction of finite products to finite sums by the monogenic powers-of-two subsemigroup, which collapses multiplication to addition of exponents.
powers of two form a one-prime cyclic multiplicative subsemigroup used positively to reduce products to sums
Powers of two are used positively to transfer finite sums to finite products, and the cyclic geometric progression is regular but not a maximal-symmetry, exceptional, extremal, or degenerate object.
The powers-of-two trick is a positive homomorphic encoding of sums as products via a very regular geometric sequence.
Powers of two are used constructively to turn products into sums, and structurally form a one-generator multiplicative subsemigroup.

examples.name: Dyadic rationals as the intermediate test case
examples.note: The known negative result for dyadic rationals serves as the motivating finite-base case whose failure to extend helps lead to the minimal-base approach.
examples.engagement_reason: dyadic rationals are defined and cited as a negative intermediate case, but no construction or calculation is carried out on them

passes.reason: dyadics are the one-prime minimal terminating-base subcase used to show the hoped positive extension already fails

the dyadic rationals are the one-prime minimal-denominator localization used as a negative test case for extending the sums-products result

the dyadic rationals Z[1/2] are a one-prime localization used as a negative test case for simultaneous sums and products.
The dyadics are cited as a domain where the sums-and-products hope already fails, and structurally they restrict denominators to one prime.

The dyadic rationals are presented as an intermediate domain where the desired sums-and-products statement still fails, and structurally they are a one-prime/minimal localization of the rationals.
Dyadic rationals are cited as a domain where the hoped-for sums-and-products statement still fails, but they form a systematic localization rather than a sporadic or degenerate object.
The dyadic rationals are cited as a domain where the sums-and-products hope still fails, and they have only one-prime denominator structure.

examples.name: Factorial-base expansion of rationals
examples.note: The factorial base is mentioned as a representation in which every rational has finite support, illustrating why mere finite expansion is not enough because addition and multiplication behave poorly there.

factorial-base expansion is a systematic finite representation of rationals mentioned as an awkward possible tool rather than a refuting instance

examples.name: Constant coloring of the rationals
examples.note: As a trivial response to a quantifier question, the constant coloring illustrates that the theorem only asserts existence of one bad coloring, not that every coloring has the obstruction.
examples.engagement_reason: the constant coloring is only given as a trivial example in response to a question

passes.reason: the constant coloring is a collapsed, coarsest, maximally homogeneous coloring refuting any universal bad-coloring reading
the constant coloring is the trivial one-color extreme, used to show the stronger universal bad-coloring claim is false
the constant coloring is the trivial maximally homogeneous coloring used to disprove a universal reading of the bounded-base obstruction
The constant coloring is the collapsed minimum-color case, maximally invariant, and is used to refute any universal version of the denominator-prime conclusion for every coloring.
The constant coloring is the trivial maximally invariant one-coloring used to refute a universal bad-coloring interpretation.
the one-color coloring is a collapsed, maximally homogeneous minimum-color case used to refute the possible for-every-coloring strengthening.
the one-coloring is the coarsest trivial maximally invariant coloring used to refute a for-every-coloring reading.

The constant coloring refutes any universal bad-coloring claim and is the collapsed one-color, maximally minimal coloring.
